content.problemStatement:
You are given an array AAA of length NNN containing the elements −1-1−1 and 111 only. Determine if it is possible to rearrange the array AAA in such a way that AiA_iAi​ is **not** the geometric mean of Ai−1A_{i-1}Ai−1​ and Ai+1A_{i+1}Ai+1​, for all iii such that 2≤i≤N−12 \leq i \leq N-12≤i≤N−1.

YYY is said to be the geometric mean of XXX and ZZZ if Y2=X⋅ZY^2 = X \cdot ZY2=X⋅Z.


Input Format

The first line contains a single integer TTT - the number of test cases. Then the test cases follow.
The first line of each test case contains an integer NNN - the size of the array AAA.
The second line of each test case contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \dots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​ denoting the array AAA.


Output Format
For each test case, output `Yes` if it is possible to rearrange AAA in such a way that AiA_iAi​ is not the geometric mean of Ai−1A_{i-1}Ai−1​ and Ai+1A_{i+1}Ai+1​, where 2≤i≤N−12 \leq i \leq N-12≤i≤N−1. Otherwise output `No`.


You may print each character of `Yes` and `No` in uppercase or lowercase (for example, `yes`, `yEs`, `YES` will be considered identical).
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Then T test cases follow. Each test case consists of two lines: the first line contains an integer N (3 ≤ N ≤ 1000) – the size of the array. The second line contains N space‑separated integers, each equal to -1 or 1.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing "Yes" if it is possible to rearrange the array so that for every i (2 ≤ i ≤ N‑1) A_i is not the geometric mean of its neighbours, otherwise output "No".

sampleTestCases.explanation: Test case 1: both 1 and -1 appear, so a valid rearrangement exists (e.g., 1 1 -1 -1 1). Test case 2: only 1 appears, impossible. Test case 3: both signs appear, so answer is Yes.
First case contains both signs → Yes. Second case contains only 1 → No.
All elements are -1, so the required alternating condition cannot be satisfied.

solution.approach:
For any three consecutive positions i‑1, i, i+1 the condition A_i^2 ≠ A_{i-1}·A_{i+1} simplifies (because A_i^2 = 1) to A_{i-1}·A_{i+1} = -1, i.e. the elements at distance two must have opposite signs. Hence for every index j, the elements at positions j and j+2 must be opposite. This forces the subsequence of all odd indices to alternate signs, and the subsequence of all even indices to alternate signs independently. Consequently, if N ≥ 3 both signs (-1 and 1) must appear in the array; otherwise the alternating requirement cannot be satisfied. The condition is also sufficient: when both signs are present we can always build a valid arrangement by choosing any sign for A_1, setting A_3 = -A_1, A_5 = A_1, … and similarly for the even positions. Therefore the answer is "Yes" iff the original array contains at least one 1 and at least one -1.

Algorithm per test case:
1. Count how many 1's (cntPos) and how many -1's (cntNeg) are present.
2. If cntPos > 0 and cntNeg > 0 output "Yes", else output "No".
The algorithm runs in O(N) time per test case and O(1) extra space.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, O(T·N) overall.
